title: Photovoltaic creation of charged domain walls in barium titanate
authors: P. S. Bednyakov, P. V. Yudin, A. K. Tagantsev, J. Hlinka
abstract: The optical control of domain structures in ferroelectrics is of great interest. In the present work, we demonstrate the reliable creation of charged domain walls - conductive channels in otherwise insulating barium titanate - by a combined effect of light and electric field. We propose a scenario for the documented process, in which the bulk photovoltaic effect plays the key role, providing charge screening at the walls. Our scenario is supported by the results of phase-field simulations. The results are of interest for future reconfigurable electronic and opto-electronic devices.
summary: This paper demonstrates how UV light and an electric field can be used to create conductive channels (charged domain walls) in insulating barium titanate. The researchers show that the bulk photovoltaic effect is the key driver for this structural transformation, offering a way to create reconfigurable electronic and opto-electronic devices.
main_problem: Understanding the mechanism behind the transformation of neutral domain walls (NDWs) into conductive charged domain walls (CDWs) in barium titanate under UV illumination and an electric field.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that the bulk photovoltaic effect (BPVE) drives the conversion of NDWs to CDWs by providing necessary charge screening at the walls, a process supported by both experimental current measurements and phase-field simulations.
method: The study combines experimental optical observation and current-voltage measurements on BTO crystals with phase-field simulations using the finite element method (COMSOL) and a Landau-Ginzburg-Devonshire framework.
model_or_system: The physical system is barium titanate (BaTiO3) single crystals with an initial structure of neutral domain walls parallel to the (110) plane, subjected to UV illumination and a DC electric field.
key_observables: Domain wall configuration (NDW vs. CDW), photocurrent (J), photovoltaic coefficient, and current-voltage (I-V) characteristics.
important_parameters: UV wavelength (365 nm), electric field strength (~4 kV/cm), light intensity (~10 mW/cm2), and time scales for CDW formation (10 minutes to 2 hours).
main_assumptions: The simulation assumes homogeneous, through-the-sample generation of photo-carriers and treats light polarization as random and uniform.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows experimental geometry and the resulting CDW stripes; Fig 2 shows the time-series evolution of domain structures; Fig 4 and 5 illustrate the schematic mechanism and stages of CDW growth; Fig 6 shows phase-field simulation results of domain evolution.
paper_structure: The paper begins with experimental setup and domain engineering, moves to the observation of current increases and domain transitions, proposes a mechanism based on the bulk photovoltaic effect, validates this via phase-field simulations, and concludes with a theoretical derivation of the photovoltaic coefficients.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1 The geometry of the experiment (a). The orientations of charged and neutral domain walls are marked with red and blue lines, respectively. The sample is illuminated with UV light and observed from the [001] direction; the electric field is applied along the [110] direction. (b) A photograph of the (001) surface of a typical sample with ferroelectric CDWs. Domain walls appear as colored stripes due to light interference when viewed slightly off from a top-down position.

Fig. 2 The (001) plane view of CDW formation. NDWs are seen as the horizontal lines in a-f, CDWs - as the vertical lines in f-h. (a) The domain struc- ture under an applied voltage of 300 V, which is identical to that initial (no UV). (b–g) The evolu- tion of the domain structure when the voltage is kept while the sample is UV illuminated. (h) Final configuration with conductive CDWs. The arrows show the direction of the spontaneous polarization.

Fig. 3 The time evolution of current during the NDW-to-CDW transformation, with the schematics of the domain structures. (a) Region 1: The voltage is applied and no illumination is present. No change in the domain structure is observed. Region 2: The voltage is kept. Under UV illumination, bending of NDWs and the formation of zigzag patterns are observed. Region 3: The system of CDWs is formed. I–V curves for the sample with numerous NDWs without UV illumination (b) and with it (c).
Fig. 4 The photovoltaic mechanism for the desta- bilization of NDWs and the growth of CDWs.

Fig. 5 Schematic for the stages of CDW growth: (a) appearance of original domain inclinations due to the asymmetry of the screening of sample edges with positive and negative free carriers; (b) growth of kinks due to the photovoltaic effect and their alignment into vertical columns; (c) formation of zigzag CDWs; (d) the final structure containing vertical CDWs. The view is taken in the same direction as in Fig. 2.
